_meta.purpose: Per-state HVAC data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages
_meta.sources: IECC 2021 climate zone map; NOAA NCEI normals (HDD/CDD); state HVAC license boards (verified URLs); BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 49-9021 HVAC mechanic state means); state utility commission heat-pump rebate programs.

fields.iecc_zone: 2021 IECC climate zone (1-8, A=moist, B=dry, C=marine)
fields.hdd_annual: Annual heating degree days (base 65F) typical for state's main population zone
fields.cdd_annual: Annual cooling degree days (base 65F) typical for state's main population zone
fields.license_status: required (state-issued license + exam) / registration (state registration only) / none (statewide; municipal may apply)
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for HVAC contractor licensing in this state
fields.permit_typical: Typical municipal permit threshold for residential HVAC replacement
fields.hvac_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 49-9021 HVAC Mechanics & Installers (USD)
fields.avg_replacement_low: Typical low end of 3-ton AC + 80%+ AFUE furnace complete replacement (USD)
fields.avg_replacement_high: Typical high end (variable-speed inverter + ductwork modifications) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific HVAC, refrigerant, or licensing rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate driver of HVAC system selection in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.license_board_name: Alabama Board of Heating, Air Conditioning, & Refrigeration Contractors (HACR)
AL.permit_typical: most cities require for full system replacement
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §34-31A-3 requires a separate HACR license for any HVAC work over $750 — and the HACR Board (not the Home Builders Licensure Board) issues residential certifications, with EPA Section 608 refrigerant certification stacked on top for any system containing controlled refrigerant.
AL.climate_concern: Gulf Coast counties carry dewpoints above 75°F from May through October, pushing latent dehumidification load to over half of total cooling load — right-sized 16-SEER2 variable-speed inverter systems outperform oversized fixed-speed AC that short-cycles and leaves humidity behind.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Mechanical Administrator (refrigerant) certification
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska Statute 18.62 requires every firm doing HVAC or refrigeration work to retain at least one Mechanical Administrator certified by the state — a unique credential not used in any other state — on top of EPA 608 refrigerant certification.
AK.climate_concern: Fairbanks design temperatures routinely hit -40°F where conventional heat pumps shut down on outdoor lockout; oil-fired Toyotomi-style monitor heaters and direct-vent oil furnaces dominate, with cold-climate inverter heat pumps only viable in Anchorage and Juneau microclimates.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license K-39 (Air Conditioning, Refrigeration, Evaporative Cooling)
AZ.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required for replacement
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona ROC license K-39 is mandatory for any cooling, refrigeration, or evaporative cooling work above $1,000, and a separate dual K-39/K-39R classification is needed for residential vs commercial — unlicensed contracting is a Class 1 misdemeanor and voids any contract claim under A.R.S. §32-1153.
AZ.climate_concern: Phoenix and Tucson see 110+ design dry-bulb temperatures with negligible humidity, so condensers must be oversized for desert-rated capacity (AHRI ratings derate sharply above 95°F outdoor) and inverter-driven variable-speed compressors run nearly continuous in July-August.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas HVACR Licensing Board — separate Class A (unlimited) and Class B (residential ≤25-ton) tiers
AR.permit_typical: varies by municipality, Little Rock + NWA require
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas HVACR Licensing Board enforces a uniquely two-tier system under Ark. Code §17-33: Class A allows unlimited tonnage and commercial work, while Class B caps installers at 25-ton residential systems and 1.5 million BTU heating — a stricter scope split than most neighboring states.
AR.climate_concern: Northwest Arkansas hail-belt frequency rivals Oklahoma — annual severe-hail probability above 4% in Benton/Washington counties means condenser hail guards or impact-rated cabinets are commonly written into homeowner insurance roof+HVAC bundles.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-20 (Warm-Air Heating, Ventilating & Air-Conditioning)
CA.distinctive_law: California SB 1206 (2018) caps refrigerant Global Warming Potential at 750 for new stationary AC systems by 2025 — phasing out R-410A faster than the federal AIM Act timeline — and Title 24 Part 6 mandates HERS-rater field verification of duct leakage and refrigerant charge on every change-out.
CA.climate_concern: California's eight climate zones span coastal 3C (Bay Area, near-zero cooling load) through inland 3B Central Valley (Fresno-Bakersfield, 4,500+ CDD) — Title 24 prescriptively allows different equipment minimums by zone, and wildland-urban-interface filtration (MERV 13+) is now standard on retrofits in CAL FIRE high-severity zones.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide HVAC license — Denver, Colorado Springs, Boulder, Aurora require local registration with separate journeyperson exams
CO.permit_typical: city mechanical permit required separately by municipality
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado Senate Bill 22-051 created a state income tax credit of up to $1,500 for residential heat pump installation — stackable with Xcel Energy and Black Hills Energy utility rebates and the federal IRA HEEHRA, making CO one of the most aggressively HP-incentivized states in the Mountain West.
CO.climate_concern: Front Range elevation (5,000-7,500 ft) imposes a 6-8% combustion derate per 1,000 feet on every gas furnace — high-altitude orifice kits are required, and evaporative coolers (swamp coolers) remain widely installed in Denver-metro single-family homes where summer dewpoints rarely exceed 50°F.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — S-1 (Unlimited) / S-2 (Limited) Heating, Piping & Cooling license
CT.permit_typical: town mechanical permit required for system replacement
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut Public Act 11-80 created the Energize Connecticut alliance and the Connecticut Green Bank, which underwrite zero-interest residential heat pump financing and rebates of $750–$15,000 per installation through Eversource and UI — the country's first state Green Bank model.
CT.climate_concern: Aging fuel-oil installed base (over 40% of homes still heat with oil — highest rate after Maine) combined with 130-mph coastal wind design loads in Fairfield and New London counties drives Energize CT's heat-pump retrofit push as oil deliveries become harder to source on hurricane-impacted shoreline.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Board of Plumbing, Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning & Refrigeration — Master/Journeyperson HVACR tiers
DE.permit_typical: county permit required (Sussex, Kent, New Castle)
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware Code Title 24 §1804 combines plumbing and HVACR oversight under a single Board, requiring a Master HVACR license to pull permits and a Journeyperson HVACR for installation work — and uniquely requires four years of journeyperson experience plus a separate exam before applying for Master status.
DE.climate_concern: Sussex County beach communities sit on the 130-mph wind line where condenser tie-down to a poured pad with hurricane-rated brackets is standard, while the propane-and-oil heating mix in Kent County drives ongoing fuel-switching to heat pumps via Delaware Sustainable Energy Utility (DESEU) Energize Delaware HP rebates.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida CILB — Class A Mechanical Contractor (unlimited) / Class B (≤25-ton, ≤500k BTU) per F.S. 489
FL.permit_typical: permit required statewide; HVHZ counties require NOA on equipment
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Energy Conservation Code Section R403 mandates a minimum duct R-6 plus pressure-tested duct leakage of 4 cfm per 100 sq ft of conditioned floor area on every change-out, and Miami-Dade and Broward HVHZ jurisdictions require a Notice of Acceptance (NOA) on every air handler and condenser installed.
FL.climate_concern: Year-round cooling-dominant load with dewpoints above 75°F daily May through October pushes latent dehumidification capacity ahead of sensible — heat pumps with variable-speed inverter compressors and ECM blowers are the dominant equipment, and HVHZ wind-load brackets anchor every condenser pad in Miami-Dade and Broward.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: Georgia Construction Industry Licensing Board — Conditioned Air Class I (unrestricted) / Class II (≤175,000 BTU)
GA.distinctive_law: O.C.G.A. §43-14 splits Georgia's HVAC license into Class I (Conditioned Air Contractor, unrestricted) and Class II (capped at 175,000 BTU heating and 25-ton cooling) — Class II is one of the lowest-tier residential-only licenses among Southeast states and bars the holder from any commercial or industrial work.
GA.climate_concern: Atlanta-metro red clay soil expansion and contraction cracks unbonded condenser pads within 5-7 years; setting the outdoor unit on a poured concrete pad with isolation mat is the regional retrofit standard, and the long humid cooling season (April through October) makes ECM variable-speed blowers a high-ROI upgrade.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Contractors License Board — C-52 (Ventilating and Air Conditioning) classification
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii Revised Statutes §444-9 issues a C-52 specialty contractor license uniquely scoped to ventilating, air-conditioning, and dehumidification — and requires every applicant to pass a separate trade exam plus four years of supervised journey-level experience under another C-52 holder.
HI.climate_concern: Salt-laden trade-wind exposure on every Hawaiian coast shortens conventional aluminum-fin coil life to under five years; sea-coast units with epoxy-coated coils, stainless screws, and corrosion-resistant copper risers are the standard upgrade, and ductless mini-splits dominate retrofits over central air.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Division of Building Safety — HVAC Contractor + Journeyperson exams under IDAPA 07.03
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho IDAPA 07.03.12 requires every HVAC firm to retain a state-licensed HVAC Contractor and pull state-issued mechanical permits — a uniquely centralized model in the Mountain West where Idaho's Division of Building Safety enforces statewide rather than delegating to municipalities.
ID.climate_concern: Sun Valley, McCall, and Coeur d'Alene sit at elevations above 6,000 feet where combustion-equipment derate exceeds 12% — high-altitude orifice kits are mandatory, and ductless mini-splits dominate retrofits in older Boise and Idaho Falls homes built without supply ductwork.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: Illinois — no statewide HVAC license; Chicago, Cook County, Naperville require municipal HVAC contractor registration with separate exams
IL.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois Public Act 102-0662 (Climate and Equitable Jobs Act, 2021) targets 100% carbon-free electricity by 2050 and directs ComEd, Ameren, and Nicor Gas to fund residential heat pump rebates of $1,000–$1,500 per ton plus low-income equity adders — the most aggressive utility HP push in the Midwest.
IL.climate_concern: Chicago's pre-1940 building stock often retains gravity-warm-air or steam radiator distribution where ducted heat pumps are infeasible without major retrofit; hybrid solutions (high-efficiency boiler + ductless heat pumps) dominate North Side and Lakefront retrofits, while suburban cookie-cutter ranches do straight gas-furnace + AC change-outs.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: Indiana — no statewide HVAC license; Indianapolis (Marion County), Fort Wayne, and Evansville require local plumbing/HVAC certification with municipal exams
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana has no statewide HVAC contractor license — installer credentialing falls entirely to municipal building departments, with Indianapolis requiring a separate plumbing/HVAC class certificate and bond, while smaller counties accept any registered general contractor for mechanical work over $1,000.
IN.climate_concern: Indiana's coal-region utility rate structure (NIPSCO, Duke, Vectren, AES Indiana) keeps natural gas heat at 30-40% of the operating cost of electric resistance heat, which makes pure heat-pump retrofits a slow sell — dual-fuel systems (HP + gas backup at a 25-35°F balance point) are the regional sweet spot.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Plumbing & Mechanical Systems Board — Master/Journeyperson Mechanical license under IAC 641-310
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Administrative Code 641-310 issues four separate Mechanical credentials — Master Mechanical, Journeyperson Mechanical, Master HVAC-Refrigeration, and Journeyperson HVAC-Refrigeration — with the HVAC-Refrigeration tracks specifically excluding hydronic boiler work, a scope split unique to Iowa.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa winter design temperatures of -20°F push 95%+ AFUE condensing gas furnaces over heat pumps for primary heat, and tornado-prone Mississippi River valley counties (Pottawattamie, Polk) impose IBC anchoring requirements on outdoor condensing units — strap-and-bolt to a poured pad with dual lag points.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: Kansas — no statewide HVAC license; Wichita, Overland Park, Topeka, KCK enforce separate municipal HVAC class registrations
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas leaves all HVAC licensing to municipalities — Wichita's Municipal Code §3.50 requires a separately-tested Class I or Class II mechanical contractor license per Sedgwick County, while Johnson County (Overland Park, Olathe) accepts MARC reciprocity from any Kansas City metro municipality with similar passing standards.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas Tornado Alley counties impose IBC outdoor-unit anchoring with hurricane straps to a poured concrete pad; sharp seasonal swings between -10°F winter design and 105°F summer design make dual-fuel (HP + gas) systems the regional sweet spot, with Westar and Evergy utility rebates favoring 16+ SEER2 equipment.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings & Construction — HVAC Master/Journeyman Mechanic license
KY.permit_typical: state-issued or local permit required for replacement
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky KRS 198B.650 establishes a tiered HVAC Master Contractor / Master Mechanic / Journeyman Mechanic licensure with a uniquely strict 4,000-hour journey requirement before sitting for the Master exam — among the longest journeyperson hour requirements in the Southeast.
KY.climate_concern: Karst topography across central Kentucky (Mammoth Cave region, Bowling Green, Bardstown) makes ground-source geothermal heat pumps unusually viable due to the shallow constant-temperature aquifer — closed-loop horizontal trenches and shallow vertical bores have payback periods 3-5 years shorter than the national average.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors (LSLBC) — Mechanical (HVAC) Specialty for jobs over $10,000
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana's $10,000 threshold for the LSLBC Mechanical Specialty license is one of the highest residential thresholds in the country — below it, a registered Home Improvement Contractor with EPA 608 certification can do replacement work, but above it the LSLBC license, $10K bond, and exam are mandatory.
LA.climate_concern: Gulf Coast dewpoints exceed 78°F from May through September, putting latent dehumidification load above sensible cooling on most design days — undersized blowers cause persistent indoor humidity above 60% even with the AC running, so right-sizing tonnage to actual Manual J load (not square footage rules of thumb) is the regional practice.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: Maine Oil & Solid Fuel Board (combustion) + Plumbing & Mechanical Examiners (HVAC/refrigerant)
ME.permit_typical: town code office mechanical permit required
ME.distinctive_law: Maine uniquely splits HVAC licensing across two boards — the Oil & Solid Fuel Board licenses combustion-equipment installers (oil furnaces, biomass) while the Plumbing & Mechanical Examiners regulate refrigerant work — and Efficiency Maine's heat-pump rebate of $2,400 per ton at 100% AMI is among the most generous in the country.
ME.climate_concern: Over 60% of Maine homes still heat with fuel oil — the highest oil-heat penetration in the U.S. — and Efficiency Maine's HP push has driven cold-climate ductless mini-splits onto more than 130,000 homes since 2014, displacing oil deliveries in coastal York and Cumberland counties faster than upstate Aroostook.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland State Board of HVACR Contractors (DLLR) — Master / Limited / Journeyman / Apprentice tiers
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland's State Board of HVACR Contractors under Business Regulation Article §9A enforces a four-tier system (Master, Limited, Journeyman, Apprentice) with a Master HVACR contractor needed to pull every commercial or residential mechanical permit — and EmPOWER Maryland funnels Pepco, BGE, and SMECO HP rebates of up to $7,500 through a single application portal.
MD.climate_concern: Chesapeake Bay watershed's high water table makes vertical-bore ground-source heat pumps unusually cost-effective (drilling cost is roughly half the New England average), and EmPOWER Maryland incentives explicitly stack with the federal IRA 25C tax credit for geothermal — payback periods regularly fall under 7 years on Eastern Shore single-family homes.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Board of State Examiners of Plumbers and Gas Fitters + Refrigeration Tech (527 CMR 12.00)
MA.distinctive_law: 527 CMR 12.00 requires a Massachusetts Refrigeration Technician License (Type 1 through 7 by tonnage and refrigerant class) for any work involving refrigerant — a credential separate from the federal EPA 608 — and Mass Save's Whole-Home Air-Source Heat Pump program covers up to 75% of an installation cost up to $10,000 per dwelling.
MA.climate_concern: Coastal Cape Cod and Berkshire County have radically different design loads (5,800 HDD coastal vs 7,500 HDD inland), and the Stretch Energy Code adopted in 280+ municipalities raised the prescriptive efficiency bar past base IECC — air-sealing and HRV/ERV ventilation are baked into Mass Save HEAT loan financing for HP retrofits.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.license_board_name: Michigan LARA — Mechanical Contractor license under Public Act 192 of 1984 with eight specialty designations
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan's Mechanical Contractor Act (Public Act 192 of 1984) splits the trade into eight specialty designations — HVAC Equipment, Hydronic Heating, Refrigeration, Boilers, Limited Heating Service, Specialty Heating, LP Distribution, and Solid Fuel — each requiring its own exam and 6,000 hours of journey-level experience.
MI.climate_concern: Lake-effect snow off Lake Michigan and Lake Superior (Marquette, Traverse City, Holland) drives ground-snow loads past 50 psf where condensers need to be elevated on stands above expected snow line, and the Upper Peninsula's 8,500+ HDD pushes propane backup for any cold-climate heat pump installation.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota DLI — Master/Journeyman Plumber required for refrigerant lines per Minn. Stat. 326B.46
MN.permit_typical: city or state mechanical permit required
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statute 326B.46 uniquely requires only a state-licensed Master or Journeyman Plumber to make and braze refrigerant connections — a scope quirk that means HVAC firms in MN must either employ a licensed plumber on staff or sub the line-set work, distinct from every other state's refrigerant licensing approach.
MN.climate_concern: Twin Cities winter design temperatures of -25°F push heat pumps past their useful lockout point on roughly 30-40 nights per year — dual-fuel systems (cold-climate HP + 95% AFUE gas furnace at a 5°F balance point) are the dominant retrofit pattern, and CenterPoint Energy's Conservation Improvement Program funds rebates of $750-$2,000 per ton.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi State Board of Contractors — Commercial Mechanical license over $50,000; residential largely municipal
MS.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi Code §31-3-15 sets one of the highest commercial mechanical licensing thresholds in the country at $50,000 — below that level, a registered general contractor with EPA 608 cert can install residential HVAC, while jobs above require a state-licensed Mechanical Contractor with $50K bond and exam.
MS.climate_concern: Mississippi Delta and Gulf Coast humidity (dewpoints over 75°F from May through October) makes oversized AC the dominant install error — short-cycling units fail to dehumidify, leaving indoor RH above 60% with chronic mold risk; Manual-J load calcs that override square-footage rules of thumb are essential for any new construction.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.license_board_name: Missouri — no statewide HVAC license; St. Louis County, Kansas City, Springfield enforce municipal mechanical contractor exams
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri has no statewide HVAC contractor license — credentialing is fully delegated to municipalities, and St. Louis County's separate Mechanical Examiners Board issues a Master Mechanical Contractor license that's reciprocal with Jackson County (Kansas City) but not with St. Louis City, creating a uniquely fragmented metro-by-metro licensure landscape.
MO.climate_concern: Tornado-belt counties (Greene, Christian, Boone, Jackson) impose IBC anchoring on outdoor condensers (strap-and-bolt to a poured pad), and the Missouri River valley humidity drives variable-speed ECM blowers — Ameren MO and Spire utility rebates favor 95%+ AFUE gas furnaces over straight heat pumps.

MT.name: Montana
MT.license_board_name: Montana — no statewide HVAC license; Bozeman, Missoula, Billings require municipal mechanical certification
MT.distinctive_law: Montana has no statewide HVAC contractor license, but Montana Department of Labor & Industry registers any contractor doing work over $2,500 — a uniquely low threshold that pulls residential HVAC change-out into the registered-contractor framework even in unlicensed municipalities.
MT.climate_concern: Continental Divide microclimates (Bozeman 4,800 ft, Missoula 3,200 ft, Billings 3,100 ft) drive 8-12% combustion derate on furnaces, and -25°F winter design temps in Eastern Plains valleys (Glasgow, Miles City) make natural gas or LP furnaces the only viable primary heat — heat pumps need cold-climate inverter compressors and propane backup.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska — no statewide HVAC license; Omaha, Lincoln, Bellevue require municipal mechanical contractor exams
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska's State Electrical Division licenses HVAC controls and electrical interlocks, but mechanical/refrigerant work itself is left to cities — Omaha's Class A Mechanical Contractor exam covers system design, while Lincoln tests the journey-level mechanic separately, requiring HVAC firms to maintain dual-municipality credentials in the Omaha metro.
NE.climate_concern: Nebraska's place inside Tornado Alley (Sarpy, Douglas, Lancaster counties) drives IBC outdoor-unit anchoring with hurricane straps to a poured concrete pad, and the Sandhills' continental climate pushes -10°F winter design with 100°F summer design — dual-fuel HP/gas systems balance the swing better than either alone.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.license_board_name: Nevada State Contractors Board — C-21 (Refrigeration & Air Conditioning) classification
NV.permit_typical: Clark County or Washoe County mechanical permit required
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada's State Contractors Board issues C-21 (Refrigeration and Air Conditioning) as a mandatory specialty classification under NRS 624 — required for any HVAC installation regardless of dollar value — with a $50,000 bond minimum and a separate trade exam administered through PSI Examination Services.
NV.climate_concern: Las Vegas summer dry-bulb temps regularly exceed 110°F where condenser AHRI ratings derate sharply (lose 15-20% capacity) — variable-speed inverter compressors with oversized condenser coils run nearly continuous from June through September, while Reno's 4,500 ft elevation imposes 6% combustion derate on every gas furnace.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.license_board_name: New Hampshire — no statewide HVAC license; municipal permit + EPA 608 cert required
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire issues no statewide HVAC contractor license (one of only a handful of states with no licensure floor), relying instead on EPA Section 608 refrigerant certification at the federal level — meaning the homeowner's primary protection against unlicensed work is the town code officer's permit-and-inspection process, not a state board.
NH.climate_concern: Over 50% of New Hampshire homes still heat with fuel oil — second-highest in the country after Maine — and NHSaves' Home Energy Assistance program rebates cold-climate heat pumps at $1,500-$2,500 per ton; the White Mountains region's 8,500+ HDD pushes any HP retrofit toward dual-fuel with propane or oil backup.

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.license_board_name: New Jersey HVACR Master Contractor Act of 2008 (P.L. 2007 c.211) — Master HVACR Contractor required per firm
NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey's HVACR Master Contractor Act of 2008 (P.L. 2007 c.211) requires every HVAC firm to retain a state-licensed Master HVACR Contractor on staff who has passed a separate trade exam and met five-year journey-level requirements — a corporate-license model unique among Mid-Atlantic states.
NJ.climate_concern: New Jersey Clean Energy Program HP rebates of $1,000-$2,000 per ton (with low-income equity adders) target the dense aging-boiler stock in Hudson, Essex, and Passaic urban cores where row-house construction makes ducted retrofits costly — ductless mini-splits and air-to-water heat pumps tied into existing radiant systems are the regional retrofit pattern.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.license_board_name: New Mexico Construction Industries Division — MM98 Mechanical Contractor classification
NM.distinctive_law: New Mexico Construction Industries Division enforces a uniquely centralized mechanical license — the MM98 classification — that covers HVAC, refrigeration, and ventilating in a single state-issued credential pulled directly from CID rather than from any municipal authority, with a uniform $1.50 per $1,000 of contract permit fee schedule statewide.
NM.climate_concern: Albuquerque and Santa Fe (5,300 and 7,200 ft elevation) sit in the dry high desert where evaporative coolers ("swamp boxes") still serve over 30% of single-family homes during the dry pre-monsoon May-June window — but monsoon humidity in July-August forces a refrigeration-grade backup, driving hybrid evap+AC dual-source designs unique to the region.

NY.name: New York
NY.license_board_name: New York — no statewide HVAC license; NYC DOB Master Plumber, Nassau/Suffolk/Westchester separate; NYS Clean Heat program HP rebates
NY.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required
NY.distinctive_law: New York City Local Law 154 of 2021 prohibits combustion appliances (including gas furnaces and boilers) in new buildings under seven stories effective 2025-12-31 and in all new construction by 2027 — accelerating heat pump adoption faster than any utility rebate program could, while NYS Clean Heat funds Con Edison and National Grid HP rebates of $1,500-$3,000 across the state.
NY.climate_concern: Adirondack and Catskill regions hit 7,500+ HDD with -20°F design temps where cold-climate inverter HPs need supplemental electric resistance or oil backup, while NYC's pre-war steam-radiator stock is the largest combustion-heating retrofit market in the country — air-to-water HPs tied into existing radiator distribution are the technical workaround Con Edison and NYSERDA are aggressively financing.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.license_board_name: NC State Board of Examiners of Plumbing, Heating & Fire Sprinkler Contractors — H1 (unrestricted) / H2 (≤15 ton) / H3 (residential dwelling)
NC.distinctive_law: North Carolina General Statutes §87-21 establishes a uniquely tiered HVAC license — H1 (unrestricted), H2 (capped at 15 ton residential and light commercial), and H3 (single-family dwellings only) — and explicitly excludes heat-pump-only work under 5 tons from H3 supervision, allowing limited installer-only credentials.
NC.climate_concern: North Carolina's three-region climate split — Outer Banks coast (3A, salt-corrosion coil failures), Piedmont 3A/4A (Charlotte-Raleigh, balanced HP-dominant load), Blue Ridge mountains (5A, 5,500+ HDD) — drives different equipment selection per region; Duke Energy Smart $aver rebates favor 16+ SEER2 variable-speed heat pumps statewide.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.license_board_name: North Dakota — no statewide HVAC license; Fargo, Bismarck, Grand Forks require municipal mechanical contractor registration
ND.distinctive_law: North Dakota leaves all HVAC contractor licensing to municipalities and relies on the federal EPA 608 program for refrigerant cert — but the State Plumbing Board enforces a separate Plumber license for any work involving water-cooled condensers or hydronic equipment, creating a unique gap between unlicensed forced-air work and licensed water-side work.
ND.climate_concern: Fargo and Grand Forks design temperatures of -25°F push 95%+ AFUE condensing gas furnaces past heat pumps as primary heat — central AC penetration sits below 65% of single-family homes statewide, the lowest in the lower 48, and many older homes still rely on window units for the brief cooling season.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.license_board_name: Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board (OCILB) — HVAC Contractor license required for commercial work
OH.distinctive_law: Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board's HVAC license is uniquely scoped to commercial work only — residential HVAC contractors operate under municipal authority — meaning Cincinnati, Cleveland, Columbus each test their own residential mechanical contractor exams, and a state HVAC license does not authorize residential change-out work without the city add-on.
OH.climate_concern: Lake Erie lake-effect snow corridor (Cleveland through Erie PA) drives 50+ psf ground snow loads where outdoor condensers must sit on stands above expected snow line; Columbia Gas, Duke Energy, and AEP Ohio rebate structures have historically favored 95%+ AFUE gas furnaces over heat pumps, slowing electrification adoption relative to neighboring Indiana and Pennsylvania.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.license_board_name: Oklahoma Construction Industries Board (CIB) — HVACR Mechanical Contractor license
OK.distinctive_law: Oklahoma CIB requires statewide HVACR Mechanical Contractor licensure under O.S. Title 59 §1850 with a tiered Journeyman/Contractor structure — and uniquely requires every contractor to carry a $5,000 surety bond plus general liability before any work over $1,000, regardless of whether the project is residential or commercial.
OK.climate_concern: Oklahoma sits in the heart of the Tornado Alley severe-hail belt — annual probability of 2-inch+ hail in Oklahoma City and Tulsa exceeds 6% — and condenser hail guards (perforated metal cages or impact-rated cabinets) are commonly bundled into homeowner's insurance roof+HVAC replacement claims after spring-storm season.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.license_board_name: Oregon Construction Contractors Board + Building Codes Division — Mechanical Contractor (Limited Energy or Full)
OR.distinctive_law: Oregon CCB licenses Mechanical Contractors under ORS 701, and the Energy Trust of Oregon provides one of the most generous heat pump incentive programs in the country — up to $5,000 instant rebate on a single ducted HP installation plus $750-$1,500 on replacing electric resistance heat — funded through Public Purpose Charges on every utility bill.
OR.climate_concern: Western Oregon's marine 4C climate (Portland, Salem, Eugene) makes heat pumps the runaway dominant retrofit — design temps rarely fall below 25°F so HPs operate near 100% of run hours — while Bend and Medford east of the Cascades hit 6,500 HDD and need cold-climate inverter HPs with electric strip backup, creating a sharp west-east technology split.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.license_board_name: Pennsylvania Home Improvement Consumer Protection Act (HICPA) registration; municipal HVAC license in Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, Allegheny County
PA.distinctive_law: Pennsylvania has no statewide HVAC contractor license — instead, the Home Improvement Consumer Protection Act (HICPA, 2009) requires all contractors doing work over $5,000 to register with the Attorney General's Office and post a $50K bond — but Philadelphia's Code §A-602 mandates a separate City HVAC Contractor license tested through L&I, and Pittsburgh's BIRT requires its own mechanical credential.
PA.climate_concern: Eastern Pennsylvania (Philadelphia, Lehigh Valley, Pocono) retains a sizable fuel-oil installed base — over 20% of homes — while Western PA (Pittsburgh, Erie) is overwhelmingly natural gas via Marcellus Shale supply; PUC Act 129 EE programs through PECO, PPL, and Duquesne Light fund $400-$1,200 HP rebates that target the oil-dependent eastern half disproportionately.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.license_board_name: Rhode Island Department of Labor & Training — Pipefitter / Refrigeration / Mechanical contractor license tiers
RI.distinctive_law: Rhode Island uniquely separates HVAC trade licensing into Pipefitter (PFI/PFII), Refrigeration/AC Contractor, and Master Mechanic credentials under RIGL 28-27 — meaning a contractor handling both gas-piping connections and refrigerant work needs at minimum two state credentials, and Rhode Island Energy (formerly National Grid RI) HP rebates of up to $10,000 are the most generous in New England relative to state size.
RI.climate_concern: Newport County and Block Island salt-air corrosion (sea-coast units with epoxy-coated coils are the regional standard), combined with Aquidneck Island's 130-mph coastal wind design, drives both equipment selection and condenser anchoring — and the RhodeMap RI plan funds shoreline HP retrofits as part of climate adaptation funding.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.license_board_name: South Carolina LLR — Residential Specialty (HVAC) license over $5,000; Mechanical Contractor for commercial
SC.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required
SC.distinctive_law: South Carolina LLR sets a $5,000 residential threshold under SC Code §40-11-410 below which a Residential Specialty (HVAC) license is needed — one of the lowest residential HVAC dollar thresholds in the Southeast — and over $5,000 in commercial work requires the full Mechanical Contractor license and exam.
SC.climate_concern: South Carolina Lowcountry (Charleston, Beaufort, Hilton Head) carries heavy salt-air exposure that requires sea-coast units with epoxy-coated coils and stainless-steel cabinet hardware on every coastal install, while Upstate humid subtropical loads (Greenville, Spartanburg) demand variable-speed inverter HPs to manage the 8-month cooling season's high latent fraction.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.license_board_name: South Dakota — no statewide HVAC license; Sioux Falls, Rapid City, Aberdeen require municipal mechanical contractor registration
SD.distinctive_law: South Dakota has no statewide HVAC contractor license — the State Plumbing Commission only licenses water-side and refrigerant-piping work — meaning forced-air-only HVAC work falls entirely under municipal jurisdiction; Sioux Falls and Rapid City accept reciprocity for North Dakota and Minnesota mechanical contractor licenses but not for each other.
SD.climate_concern: Sioux Falls and Aberdeen winter design of -25°F push 95%+ AFUE gas furnaces past heat pumps as primary heat, and the Black Hills' 4,500 ft elevation imposes a 5-6% combustion derate — propane backup is standard on any cold-climate HP retrofit in Lawrence and Pennington counties west of the Missouri River.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.license_board_name: Tennessee Board for Licensing Contractors — HVAC contractor designation; jobs over $25,000 require state license
TN.distinctive_law: Tennessee's Board for Licensing Contractors sets a $25,000 threshold under T.C.A. §62-6-103 — among the highest residential-trigger thresholds in the Southeast — below which a contractor with HVAC scope listed on a Home Improvement registration can do change-outs, while above $25K the BC-A license, $50K bond, and full exam are required.
TN.climate_concern: Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) EnergyRight rebates of $250-$1,000 per ton make heat pumps unusually attractive in TVA service territory (most of Middle Tennessee, East Tennessee, and parts of West) — combined with already-mild winter design temps of 15-20°F, HP penetration in TN is among the highest in the South outside of Florida.

TX.name: Texas
TX.license_board_name: Texas Department of Licensing & Regulation (TDLR) — Air Conditioning & Refrigeration Contractor (Class A unlimited / Class B ≤25 ton)
TX.distinctive_law: Texas TDLR Class A and Class B Air Conditioning & Refrigeration licenses are mandated under Texas Occupations Code Chapter 1302 with strict scope limits — Class B caps at 25-ton single-system tonnage and 1.5 million BTU heating, prohibiting commercial work — and post-Winter-Storm-Uri (2021), TDLR added a new Endorsement A pathway recognizing emergency dual-fuel design experience.
TX.climate_concern: The February 2021 Uri freeze event (and 2022 December storm) exposed widespread heat-pump-only systems lacking emergency backup heat strips — Texas now sees aggressive dual-fuel HP + electric resistance backup or HP + gas furnace adoption statewide; Gulf Coast latent load remains the bigger annual driver, with Houston and Corpus Christi dewpoints over 75°F May-October.

UT.name: Utah
UT.license_board_name: Utah DOPL — E-200 General Mechanical Contractor license
UT.permit_typical: city or county mechanical permit required
UT.distinctive_law: Utah DOPL issues an E-200 General Mechanical Contractor classification under Utah Code §58-55 covering HVAC, refrigeration, plumbing, and process piping in a single broad license — a more bundled scope than most western states — with a separate E-201 (Boiler Installation) for hydronic-only work.
UT.climate_concern: Wasatch Front winter inversions trap PM2.5 in Salt Lake and Utah valleys for weeks in December-January, making MERV 13+ filtration and HEPA in-line filters more common upgrades than elsewhere; high-altitude (4,500-7,000 ft) combustion derate on furnaces ranges from 6-12%, and Rocky Mountain Power Wattsmart HP rebates favor variable-speed inverter equipment.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.license_board_name: Vermont Plumber's Examining Board (refrigerant) + Office of Professional Regulation
VT.distinctive_law: Vermont's Plumber's Examining Board uniquely licenses Refrigeration Technicians for any refrigerant work under 26 V.S.A. §2191 — and Efficiency Vermont's HP incentive of up to $4,000 per home for cold-climate ductless or ducted heat pumps drives the highest per-capita HP installation rate among the lower 48 states.
VT.climate_concern: Vermont's 7,500+ HDD across most population zones combined with one of the highest fuel-oil dependencies in the country (over 40% of homes) makes it the leading state for cold-climate HP retrofits per capita; the Champlain Valley's milder microclimate sees ducted central HPs while the Northeast Kingdom retains oil and propane backup on every HP install.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.license_board_name: Virginia DPOR — HVAC Class A (unlimited) / Class B (≤$120K project) / Class C (≤$10K project)
VA.distinctive_law: Virginia DPOR sets uniquely tiered project-value caps for HVAC contractor licensing under Va. Code §54.1-1100 — Class C limited to $10,000 single project, Class B to $120,000 single project, Class A unlimited — and the Virginia Clean Economy Act of 2020 directs Dominion and Appalachian Power to fund residential HP rebates as part of mandated energy efficiency programs.
VA.climate_concern: Virginia's three-region climate (coastal Tidewater 4A, Piedmont 4A, mountain 5A) drives different equipment selection by region — Norfolk and Virginia Beach on the 130-mph coastal wind line need anchored condensers and corrosion-resistant coils, while Southwest Virginia mountain valleys hit 5,500 HDD where dual-fuel HPs are the regional standard.

WA.name: Washington
WA.license_board_name: Washington L&I — Electrical Specialty 06A (HVAC/Refrigeration) + General Contractor registration
WA.permit_typical: L&I or local mechanical permit required
WA.distinctive_law: Washington's State Building Code Council adopted code amendments effective 2024-07 (originally HB 1390 with subsequent revisions) that prescriptively favor heat pumps in new residential construction by setting compliance paths that exclude resistance-only and fossil-fuel-only systems from the energy-credit pathway — and the WA HB 1050 phase-down of high-GWP refrigerants moves WA past the federal AIM Act timeline.
WA.climate_concern: Western Washington's marine 4C climate (Seattle, Tacoma, Olympia) makes heat pumps the dominant retrofit choice — winter design temps rarely below 25°F give HPs near-100% run hours — but Cascades-east continental valleys (Spokane, Yakima, Wenatchee) hit 6,500 HDD and 95°F+ summers, requiring cold-climate HPs with electric resistance backup and oversized condenser coils for the dry-bulb summer load.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.license_board_name: West Virginia Division of Labor — HVAC Technician/Contractor license under WV Code §29-3D
WV.distinctive_law: West Virginia Code §29-3D requires HVAC Technicians and Contractors to be licensed by the WV Division of Labor — uniquely housed under Labor rather than a Construction Industry Board — with separate continuing-education requirements and mandatory EPA 608 stacked credentials renewed every two years.
WV.climate_concern: Appalachian coal-region housing stock retains a sizable share of electric resistance heating (over 20% of homes, second-highest among lower 48 after parts of the Carolinas) where heat pump retrofits show the steepest energy-cost paybacks — Appalachian Power and Mon Power utility rebates target this electric-resistance-to-HP conversion pathway specifically.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.license_board_name: Wisconsin Department of Safety & Professional Services — HVAC credential under SPS 305
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin Administrative Code SPS 305 issues HVAC credential tiers (Master, Journey, and Apprentice) under the state Department of Safety & Professional Services — a uniquely combined Plumbing/HVAC regulatory body — and Focus on Energy provides cash incentives of $400-$1,200 per ton for heat-pump installations through every WE Energies, Alliant, and Madison Gas & Electric customer.
WI.climate_concern: Milwaukee, Madison, and Green Bay's pre-1940 housing stock includes large numbers of gravity-warm-air furnaces with massive ductwork still in service — full heat-pump retrofits often require complete duct redesign for the smaller-tonnage HP airflow patterns, while rural Northern WI's 8,500+ HDD pushes propane or natural-gas backup on every cold-climate HP install.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.license_board_name: Wyoming — no statewide HVAC license; Cheyenne, Casper, Laramie, Jackson require municipal mechanical contractor registration
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming has no statewide HVAC contractor license — the Department of Workforce Services does not credential mechanical work — and municipal models vary widely: Cheyenne requires a separately-tested Master Mechanical exam, while Jackson and Casper accept any registered general contractor for mechanical work, creating one of the loosest licensure environments in the Mountain West.
WY.climate_concern: Cheyenne (6,000 ft), Casper (5,150 ft), and Jackson (6,200 ft) sit at high elevation where combustion-equipment derate exceeds 10% and -20°F winter design temps make natural-gas or LP furnaces the only practical primary heat — heat pumps remain niche and require cold-climate inverter compressors paired with significant electric resistance or fossil-fuel backup.
